orders.grain: one row per order (duplicates on order_id are collapsed to one)

columns.name: order_id
columns.description: unique order identifier; the join key for order_lines

columns.name: order_date
columns.description: when the order was placed - drives the ISO-week revenue and order-count KPIs

columns.name: region
columns.description: sales region - used for week-over-week driver attribution

columns.name: customer
columns.description: customer name - used for driver attribution and revenue concentration

columns.name: status
columns.description: order status; 'delivered', 'shipped' or 'closed' count as fulfilled

columns.name: promised_date
columns.description: the date the order was promised to ship

columns.name: actual_ship_date
columns.description: when it actually shipped; on-time = actual_ship_date <= promised_date. NULL means NOT yet shipped - such a late order is not counted against on-time %

columns.name: net_total
columns.description: order net total - this is the revenue measure

examples: SELECT customer, SUM(net_total) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY customer ORDER BY revenue DESC
SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS orders FROM orders WHERE status IN ('delivered','shipped','closed') GROUP BY region

columns.name: order_id
columns.description: the order this line belongs to (joins to orders.order_id)

columns.name: item_code
columns.description: the stock item on the line (joins to inventory.item_code)

columns.name: qty
columns.description: quantity ordered - summed over recent weeks to estimate weekly demand

examples: SELECT l.item_code, SUM(l.qty) AS units FROM order_lines l JOIN orders o ON o.order_id = l.order_id GROUP BY l.item_code ORDER BY units DESC

inventory.grain: one row per item (quantities summed if the source splits by warehouse)

columns.name: stock_qty
columns.description: units on hand - divided by average weekly demand to get weeks of cover

examples: SELECT item_code, stock_qty FROM inventory WHERE stock_qty = 0

receivables.grain: one row per open invoice (positive, dated balances)

columns.name: customer
columns.description: the customer who owes the balance

columns.name: due_date
columns.description: when payment is due; overdue days = report date - due_date

columns.name: open_amount
columns.description: the open balance; only positive, dated balances are aged

examples: SELECT customer, SUM(open_amount) AS owed FROM receivables GROUP BY customer ORDER BY owed DESC

notes: Query only these canonical entities and columns through the guarded, read-only path (the MCP `query` tool). A semantic profile maps them to a specific ERP's real tables; the raw schema is never exposed. Optional entities appear only when a profile maps them. Every access is read-only and audited.
